evidence: pom.xml uses spring-boot-starter-parent for version management — appropriate.
google-api-client 2.7.2 is used for GoogleIdTokenVerifier — correct purpose-built library.
spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client provides both server-side Google and Microsoft OAuth flows.
spring-oauth2-resource-server (via NimbusJwtDecoder) is used for Microsoft JWT decoding — appropriate.
jjwt 0.12.6 (api + impl + jackson) provides internal JWT minting/parsing — matches usage.
spring-boot-starter-data-redis provides Redis integration for auth code store and challenge store.
H2 + testcontainers postgresql for testing — appropriate test dependency management.
No duplicate library purpose detected (e.g., no two JWT libraries).
Frontend: @react-oauth/google for Google one-tap, @azure/msal-browser for Microsoft MSAL — correct purpose-built SDK choices.
Frontend: react-router-dom for routing, vite for build — all appropriate.

dependency_health.dimension_character: Both backend and frontend dependency choices are well-matched to use cases with no duplicate-purpose libraries. Backend uses the Spring ecosystem coherently with appropriate third-party additions. Frontend uses official provider SDKs for auth.
dependency_health.score_rationale: Score 78: Clean dependency choices with no conflicts. Minor concern: google-api-client is a relatively heavy library used only for ID token verification; Spring OAuth2 resource server could handle this at the framework level, but this is a minor optimization, not a defect.

add.description: Backend uses dedicated libraries for each concern (Google API client for ID token verification, MSAL via spring-oauth2 for Microsoft, jjwt for internal JWT). No library duplication for the same purpose.
